text: There's a particular thing I keep coming back to: the way condensation beads on the outside of a cold glass on a warm day. You pour something cold, set it down, and within a minute the glass has quietly drawn a fine fur of water out of the air, droplets so small they look more like frost or breath than liquid. Then they begin to find each other. Two tiny beads touch and merge into one that's heavier, and that heavier one eventually loses its grip and slides down the side, leaving a clean track behind it, and at the bottom a little ring of water spreads out on the table. What I find lovely about it is that it's a whole small weather system happening on the surface of an ordinary glass, the same physics that makes dew on grass and clouds in the sky, just shrunk down to something you can watch over the course of a conversation. Nobody arranges it and nobody is meant to notice it, which is part of why it feels like a small gift when you do. There's also something pleasing about the contradiction of it, that a glass can be wet on the outside without anything having spilled, water appearing as if from nowhere, drawn quietly out of the air just because something cold sat still for a while.
